text.name: cs_hud_first_run
text.text: Crop Moisture Monitor is now active. Press Shift+M to toggle the field status panel.

text.name: cs_alert_critical
text.text: Field %d: %s at drought stress threshold — irrigate NOW!

text.name: cs_favor_soil_sample
text.text: Take a soil sample from Field %d

text.name: cs_favor_irr_check
text.text: Inspect the irrigation system near Field %d

text.name: cs_irr_no_covered_fields
text.text: No fields covered by this system.

text.name: cs_rec_urgent
text.text: URGENT: Field %d at %.0f%% moisture — irrigate immediately!

text.name: cs_rec_warning
text.text: Field %d at %.0f%% moisture — irrigation recommended within 24h.

text.name: cs_rec_ok
text.text: All fields within acceptable moisture range.

text.name: cs_rec_no_systems
text.text: No irrigation systems are currently active.

text.name: cs_pf_moisture_overlay_desc
text.text: Displays current soil moisture levels across your fields. Red indicates dry soil, green indicates optimal moisture, and blue indicates saturated soil.

text.name: cs_settings_enabled_long
text.text: Toggle the entire Seasonal Crop Stress system on or off

text.name: cs_settings_difficulty_long
text.text: Adjust stress accumulation and evapotranspiration rates

text.name: cs_settings_hud_long
text.text: Show or hide the moisture overlay on the field list

text.name: cs_settings_evap_long
text.text: Base rate of moisture loss from soil

text.name: cs_settings_yield_loss_long
text.text: Maximum percentage of yield that can be lost to drought stress

text.name: cs_settings_threshold_long
text.text: Moisture level below which stress accumulates

text.name: cs_settings_irr_costs_long
text.text: Enable or disable financial costs for running irrigation systems

text.name: cs_settings_alerts_long
text.text: Show warning notifications when fields need water

text.name: cs_settings_cooldown_long
text.text: Minimum hours between alerts for the same field

text.name: helpLine_cs_p1_intro_text
text.text: Seasonal Crop Stress is a deep simulation mod that makes water management a central part of every growing season. Every field on your farm tracks its own soil moisture level in real time, from 0% (bone dry) to 100% (saturated). Moisture rises when it rains and falls through evapotranspiration — the natural process by which heat and wind dry out the soil. During critical growth stages, crops that do not receive enough water begin to suffer: root uptake slows, grain fill is reduced, and the final harvest is smaller. This moisture deficit is tracked as accumulated stress and directly reduces the yield you collect at harvest time. With the right irrigation infrastructure and scheduling, it is fully preventable.

text.name: helpLine_cs_p1_moisture_title
text.text: The Moisture Cycle at a Glance

text.name: helpLine_cs_p1_moisture_text
text.text: Rain adds moisture to your fields, and every in-game hour that moisture evaporates at a rate determined by the current temperature, the season, and your field's soil type. Sandy soil loses moisture quickly — sometimes three times faster than clay. In winter, evaporation is minimal and fields hold water for weeks. In a summer heat wave, a loamy field can drop from 60% down to the critical threshold in just a few in-game days. Watching your moisture levels is not just about today — it is about planning a week ahead so your crops are never caught short during the growth windows that matter most for final yield.

text.name: helpLine_cs_p1_start_text
text.text: Press Shift+M to open the Moisture HUD panel on your screen. This panel lists every field on your farm with its current moisture percentage, crop type, and a colour-coded status bar. Green means your field is in good shape and no action is needed right now. Yellow means moisture is dropping and you should start thinking about irrigation soon. Red means the crop is under active drought stress — yield loss is already accumulating and you need to act immediately. This panel is your primary daily tool for keeping track of your farm's water health throughout the growing season.

text.name: helpLine_cs_p1_tips_text
text.text: Here are the most important steps to take when you first load this mod. First, open the Moisture HUD with Shift+M and scan your fields to see which soil types you have — sandy farms need irrigation infrastructure sooner than clay farms. Second, open the Crop Consultant with Shift+C to learn which fields are entering their critical growth windows soon. Third, buy and place at least one Water Pump near a river, lake, or pond, then place a Center Pivot or Drip Line to cover your most vulnerable fields. Finally, press Shift+I to open the schedule dialog and set irrigation to run overnight — cooler air means lower evaporation and more moisture retained in the soil by morning.

text.name: helpLine_cs_p2_how_text
text.text: Each field maintains its own moisture level that updates every in-game hour. When it rains, moisture rises based on rainfall intensity and how quickly your soil type absorbs water. When it is dry, moisture falls at a rate determined by temperature, season, and soil properties — a process called evapotranspiration. At 15 degrees Celsius, the base evaporation is relatively gentle. But for every degree above 15, the rate increases by 3%, meaning a 35-degree summer day evaporates moisture roughly 60% faster than a mild spring morning. Moisture is always capped between 0% and 100%, and the mod does not apply flooding damage — but a field at 100% gains no benefit from additional rain either.

text.name: helpLine_cs_p2_soil_text
text.text: Sandy soil has very high drainage and low water retention. It absorbs rain quickly but loses moisture just as fast — a sandy field can drop from healthy to critical in as little as two in-game days during a summer heat wave. Sandy farms benefit most from drip irrigation systems that deliver water steadily rather than in large occasional doses. Clay soil is the opposite: it drains slowly and holds water for a long time, making it naturally more resilient to drought but prone to staying too wet after prolonged rain. Loamy soil sits between the two extremes, with moderate absorption and evaporation rates that make it the most forgiving soil type to farm on. Your field's soil type is shown in the HUD panel and in the Crop Consultant field list at all times.

text.name: helpLine_cs_p2_seasons_text
text.text: Seasons dramatically affect how fast your soil dries out, and understanding this helps you plan your irrigation investment. In spring, temperatures are mild and evaporation runs at about 80% of the base rate — fields hold moisture well and rainfall is reasonably frequent. Summer is the most challenging season: the evaporation multiplier rises to 1.4 times the base rate, meaning fields dry out 40% faster than in spring while rainfall becomes sparser. This is when irrigation is most critical and most expensive to run without planning. Autumn is similar to spring in moisture behaviour, with moderate evaporation and more frequent rain as temperatures fall. In winter, the evaporation rate drops to just 20% of the base — fields stay moist through the cold months and irrigation is almost never necessary. Plan and place your irrigation infrastructure before summer arrives, not after the drought has already started.

text.name: helpLine_cs_p2_reading_text
text.text: When reading moisture percentages, the following thresholds are the most important to know. Above 40%: your field is in the safe zone and crops are not accumulating stress, even during their critical growth window — you can relax for now. Between 20% and 40%: the field is in warning territory. Stress is not building yet if the crop is outside its critical window, but you should schedule irrigation soon to avoid a crisis. Below 20% during a critical growth window: the crop is actively accumulating drought stress and yield loss is being recorded every hour — act immediately. Below 10%: severe stress at the maximum possible rate of yield loss. Your target is to keep all actively growing fields above 40% during their critical window, and above 20% at all other times.

text.name: helpLine_cs_p3_window_text
text.text: Every crop has a defined critical growth window — the portion of its growth cycle where moisture is most essential for final yield. Wheat is most sensitive during grain fill in spring, roughly growth stages 5 through 7. Corn has its critical window during tasselling and ear fill, which falls in the heat of summer at stages 4 through 6. Barley is similar to wheat with a slightly earlier and shorter window. Canola is vulnerable during pod fill in autumn, typically at stages 5 through 6. Soybeans are sensitive throughout the summer period with a peak during pod development. The Crop Consultant will always tell you whether any currently growing crops are inside their critical window right now, and the HUD shows each field's current risk level relative to its crop's window.

text.name: helpLine_cs_p3_accumulation_text
text.text: Stress accumulates gradually over time rather than all at once. Each in-game hour that a field spends below the critical moisture threshold adds a small amount of stress to that field's total. The rate of accumulation depends on how far below the threshold moisture has fallen: a field at 15% moisture under a critical crop will accumulate stress much faster than a field sitting at 38% just below the 40% safe zone. Stress is stored as an internal value from 0.0 to 1.0, where 1.0 represents a full season of severe drought. Crucially, stress does not reset between growth stages — it carries forward until harvest. This is why catching a drought early is so much more effective than trying to irrigate at the last minute when harvest is close.

text.name: helpLine_cs_p3_yield_text
text.text: At harvest time, accumulated stress directly reduces the amount of grain or produce collected from that field. A field with zero stress delivers its full theoretical yield. A field with low stress — around 0.2 on the internal stress scale — loses roughly 12% of its potential harvest. At moderate stress of 0.5, you lose around 30%. At maximum stress of 1.0, you lose 60% of the harvest. The yield reduction is calculated at the exact moment your harvesting machine works the field, so there is no penalty for stress that built up on a previous crop — each new growing season starts completely fresh. The current yield loss estimate is always visible in the HUD panel and in the Crop Consultant risk summary so you are never surprised at harvest time.

text.name: helpLine_cs_p3_prevention_text
text.text: The best strategy for preventing stress is to monitor the Moisture HUD and Crop Consultant daily during the growing season, and to have irrigation infrastructure in place before critical windows open. Do not wait until a field turns red — by then stress has already begun accumulating and some yield loss is inevitable. Instead, aim to keep fields in the yellow range (25-40%) topped up with scheduled overnight irrigation so they enter each day firmly in the green zone. If a sudden heat wave causes multiple fields to drop fast, use the Irrigate Now button in the irrigation schedule dialog to trigger an immediate watering session that bypasses the weekly schedule. A single overnight irrigation run is usually enough to bring a warning-level field back into the safe zone.

text.name: helpLine_cs_p4_hud_text
text.text: The Moisture HUD panel is your at-a-glance view of every field on your farm. Press Shift+M to toggle it on or off at any time — the panel is non-blocking and can stay visible while you drive vehicles and work your fields normally. The panel shows each field number, its current crop type, a moisture bar with the percentage from 0 to 100, and a coloured status label. Fields are sorted by risk level, with the most critical fields always at the top of the list, so you never have to scroll through to find trouble. The panel also displays your current season and an in-game time indicator so you can quickly orient yourself. Below the field list, a compact forecast section shows the 5-day moisture trajectory for your most at-risk fields.

text.name: helpLine_cs_p4_status_text
text.text: Each field entry in the HUD is colour-coded by its current moisture status, and understanding these colours is the most important habit you will build with this mod. A bright green entry means moisture is above 40% and the crop is not under any stress — no action needed today. A yellow or amber entry means moisture has dropped below 40% and is heading towards the danger zone, or the crop is approaching its critical growth window — plan irrigation within the next day or two. A red entry is the most urgent situation: moisture has fallen below 20% while the crop is inside its critical growth window, meaning drought stress is actively accumulating this hour and yield loss is locked in — irrigate immediately. A grey entry means the field has no crop currently planted, so moisture is tracked but no stress is accumulating.

text.name: helpLine_cs_p4_forecast_text
text.text: Each field entry in the HUD includes a compact 5-day moisture forecast shown as a small trend indicator. The forecast takes into account current weather conditions, cloud coverage, rainfall probability based on the season, and your existing irrigation schedules. The first two forecast days are weighted by current cloud coverage — if skies are clearing, the forecast reflects lower rain probability for the near future. Days three through five use seasonal average rainfall data as there is no reliable game API for multi-day weather forecasting. Treat the forecast as a planning guide rather than a guarantee: an unexpected rain event or sudden heat wave will change real outcomes. Use the forecast to identify fields trending downward over the next few days so you can act proactively rather than reactively.

text.name: helpLine_cs_p4_keys_text
text.text: The mod adds three keyboard shortcuts that can all be rebound in the game's control settings under the Seasonal Crop Stress category. Shift+M toggles the Moisture HUD panel on and off — use this to check on your fields at any time without opening any dialogs. Shift+I opens the Irrigation Schedule dialog for the nearest or currently selected irrigation system — use this to review or change your irrigation schedule, or to trigger an emergency Irrigate Now watering session. Shift+C opens the Crop Consultant dialog, which provides a ranked risk summary of your highest-risk fields, specific advice for the next 24 hours, and a 3-day moisture forecast for the most vulnerable field.

text.name: helpLine_cs_p4_reposition_text
text.text: You can move the HUD panel anywhere on your screen to avoid blocking your view of the world. Hold the right mouse button to enter Edit Mode — the panel border will turn orange to indicate it is now moveable. While holding the right mouse button, click and drag with the left mouse button to slide the panel to a new position on screen. Release both buttons to confirm the new position. To exit Edit Mode without moving, release only the right mouse button and the panel stays where it was. The panel position is saved automatically when you exit Edit Mode and persists across game sessions — the panel will reappear in exactly the same spot the next time you load this savegame. You can reposition it again at any time.

text.name: helpLine_cs_p5_types_text
text.text: Three types of irrigation infrastructure are available in the build menu under the Irrigation category. The Center Pivot covers a large circular area of up to 200 metres radius around its centre point, making it ideal for large open fields. It draws water from a connected Water Pump and can run on a set schedule or be triggered manually. The Drip Irrigation Line provides targeted coverage along a field edge or row — it is more efficient per square metre than a pivot and works well for irregularly shaped fields or narrow strips. The Water Pump is not an irrigation device itself, but a required support structure: it connects to a nearby water source and supplies water to any pivot or drip line within range. You must place at least one Water Pump before any other irrigation system will function.

text.name: helpLine_cs_p5_placement_text
text.text: To set up irrigation, start by placing a Water Pump within 500 metres of a natural water source — a river, lake, pond, or water tower. The pump will automatically detect and connect to the nearest water body. Next, place a Center Pivot or Drip Irrigation Line on or near the field you want to water. Once placed, the system will appear on your field list in the Irrigation Manager as soon as it detects it is within range of an active Water Pump. If an irrigation system shows as Disconnected in the dialog, it means either no Water Pump is nearby or the pump cannot reach a valid water source. Move the pump closer to the water or relocate it to a spot with more direct access to resolve this. Multiple irrigation systems can share a single Water Pump as long as they are all within its supply range.

text.name: helpLine_cs_p5_schedule_text
text.text: Select an irrigation system and press Shift+I to open the Irrigation Schedule dialog for that system. The dialog shows which fields are covered, the system's current status, flow rate, efficiency, and running cost per hour. To set a schedule, use the day selector to choose which days of the week the system should run, then set the daily start and end time. A typical setup is overnight from 22:00 to 06:00: temperatures are coolest at night, which means evaporation is at its lowest and the moisture you add to the soil stays there through the morning. Click Save Schedule to store your settings. The Irrigate Now button triggers an immediate one-time watering of all covered fields right now, bypassing the schedule entirely — use this during emergency droughts when you cannot wait for the next scheduled run.

text.name: helpLine_cs_p5_costs_text
text.text: Running irrigation systems has a financial cost representing electricity, fuel, and maintenance. The cost is deducted from your farm account each in-game hour that a system is actively running. A standard Center Pivot costs approximately 15 to 25 dollars per hour depending on its flow rate and condition. Drip lines are cheaper to run per hour but cover a smaller area, so plan your coverage accordingly. If you prefer to focus on the gameplay challenge without the financial element, you can disable irrigation costs entirely in the game settings under the Seasonal Crop Stress section. If FS25_UsedPlus is installed, equipment condition affects efficiency — a heavily worn pump delivers less water per hour and may not be worth the running cost until it is serviced or replaced.

text.name: helpLine_cs_p5_farmtips_text
text.text: A few practical tips for running irrigation efficiently across a large farm. First, prioritise your sandy fields for infrastructure investment — they are the first to hit critical moisture levels and the most expensive to neglect. Second, schedule irrigation to run overnight so it does not interfere with daytime field operations, and so the cooler morning air preserves the moisture benefit for as long as possible. Third, do not over-irrigate: bringing a field above 80% provides no additional crop benefit and wastes money on running costs. The Crop Consultant will flag any field that is already well-hydrated and does not need attention. Fourth, during wet seasons like spring and winter, consider pausing your irrigation schedules altogether — natural rainfall is often sufficient, and running irrigation during a rain event wastes fuel and money with no benefit to the field.

text.name: helpLine_cs_p6_what_text
text.text: Press Shift+C to open the Crop Consultant dialog at any time. The dialog provides a ranked risk summary of the five highest-risk fields on your farm, sorted by their current moisture level relative to their crop's critical threshold. For each field you see the field number, crop type, current moisture percentage, estimated yield loss accumulated so far this season, and a plain-language status label. Below the field list, a recommendation section gives you specific actionable advice for the next 24 hours — for example, exactly which field needs immediate irrigation, or confirmation that all fields are safe to leave until tomorrow. A 3-day moisture forecast for the top at-risk field is also shown so you can judge whether conditions are likely to improve or continue deteriorating.

text.name: helpLine_cs_p6_risk_text
text.text: The risk ranking shown in the Consultant is calculated from two factors: how far below the safe moisture threshold a field currently is, and how much stress has already accumulated this growing season. A field with 35% moisture and zero accumulated stress has a relatively low risk score — it is below the 40% safe zone but has not yet entered its critical window and recovery is straightforward. A field with 22% moisture and already significant accumulated stress has a high risk score because it is losing yield every hour and the season is advancing. The yield loss estimate displayed in the HUD and Consultant is a live projection based on stress accumulated so far this season — it does not account for future stress that may or may not occur before harvest.

text.name: helpLine_cs_p6_act_text
text.text: Fields labelled CRITICAL in the Consultant are your highest priority right now. These fields are below 20% moisture while their crop is inside its critical growth window — yield loss is accumulating every hour and the longer you wait, the more harvest you lose. For these fields, use the Open Irrigation Manager button at the bottom of the Consultant dialog to immediately open the schedule for the system covering that field, then click Irrigate Now to begin an emergency watering session. Fields labelled WARNING are losing moisture and approaching their critical window but are not yet accumulating yield loss — you have 24 to 48 in-game hours to act before the situation becomes critical. Fields labelled OK are in good shape and no action is required today.

text.name: helpLine_cs_p6_npc_text
text.text: If the optional FS25_NPCFavor mod is installed alongside this mod, Alex Chen will appear as an Agronomist NPC in your game world. Alex works as a consultant character with a relationship level that grows as you complete favors and act on recommendations. At low relationship levels, Alex provides the same advice you see in the standard Crop Consultant dialog. At higher relationship levels, Alex unlocks early drought warnings that fire before moisture reaches the critical threshold — giving you more reaction time before yield loss begins. At maximum relationship, Alex provides specialist irrigation scheduling advice tailored to your specific fields and soil types. Favors from Alex include tasks like taking a soil sample from a specific field, inspecting irrigation infrastructure, or making an emergency irrigation run before sundown.

text.name: helpLine_cs_p6_multi_text
text.text: In multiplayer, the soil moisture simulation runs on the host server and is automatically shared with all connected players. All players see the same moisture levels, field risk data, and HUD information. The Crop Consultant shows the same risk summary to all players. Any player can manage irrigation systems using their own Shift+I keybinding. Moisture data is sent to clients when they first connect so everyone is immediately up to date. The HUD panel position is a personal client-side preference — each player can position their HUD independently without affecting anyone else's view. Settings like difficulty, evaporation rate, and maximum yield loss are host-controlled and apply to the whole session for all players.
